coredata.dc:description: Plant growth in space goes beyond nutrition, it enhances well-being by connecting humans to nature through biophilic design. Nutrient stress is a major constrain in Space. This study used barley as a model plant in non-circulating hydroponics with varying nitrogen levels to simulate space nutrient stress conditions and test nano-fertiliser technology. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were applied in solution (hydroponics) and sprayed (aeroponics) to alleviate stress and keep the plant attractive under Space stress. The leaves' greenness was assessed using the soil plant analysis development (SPAD) during plant growth. The study found that plants grown under ‘high nitrogen’ (1 mM) conditions had higher SPAD and shoots-to-roots ratio (S/R) could reach as high as 31, and 3.2, respectively. In contrast, ‘low nitrogen’ grown plants were less green and bushy, wherein the SPAD and S/R were 11.2 and 1.03, respectively. The foliar fertilisation (FF) treatment turned out to be effective for the biophilic parameters, provided it started early, i.e. after one-week of plant growth. Applying an FF after two weeks was found to be less effective because the fresh S/R increased from 1.93 to 2.94. The FF treatment slightly increased the SPAD for the ‘low nitrogen’ sample by comparison to the control sample (no FF). Regardless of the treatment, the fresh shoot weight decreased under ‘low nitrogen’, except when being boosted by a high concentration (100 mg/l) of the nano-fertiliser quantum dots. Nonetheless, the fresh shoot weight of ‘low nitrogen’ growth was almost identical to ‘high nitrogen’ growth, which were 1.88 g and 2.20 g, respectively. These findings epitomise the potential of the nano fertiliser to reduce nitrogen use in plants. The results from the best experimental data gathered (greenness, shoots/roots, leaf number/shape) were implemented in a visualisation of an early-stage idea for the Spaceship interior using an artificial intelligence (AI) SDXL 1.0 model. Plant growth experiments in a home-built spacecraft replica confirmed that the use of LN (0.5 mM) conditions can be favourably used under ‘real-space conditions’ and that the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanofertiliser is effective for creating biophilic SPAD value.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plant growth in space goes beyond nutrition, it enhances well-being by connecting humans to nature through biophilic design. Nutrient stress is a major constrain in Space. This study used barley as a model plant in non-circulating hydroponics with varying nitrogen levels to simulate space nutrient stress conditions and test nano-fertiliser technology. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were applied in solution (hydroponics) and sprayed (aeroponics) to alleviate stress and keep the plant attractive under Space stress. The leaves' greenness was assessed using the soil plant analysis development (SPAD) during plant growth. The study found that plants grown under ‘high nitrogen’ (1 mM) conditions had higher SPAD and shoots-to-roots ratio (S/R) could reach as high as 31, and 3.2, respectively. In contrast, ‘low nitrogen’ grown plants were less green and bushy, wherein the SPAD and S/R were 11.2 and 1.03, respectively. The foliar fertilisation (FF) treatment turned out to be effective for the biophilic parameters, provided it started early, i.e. after one-week of plant growth. Applying an FF after two weeks was found to be less effective because the fresh S/R increased from 1.93 to 2.94. The FF treatment slightly increased the SPAD for the ‘low nitrogen’ sample by comparison to the control sample (no FF). Regardless of the treatment, the fresh shoot weight decreased under ‘low nitrogen’, except when being boosted by a high concentration (100 mg/l) of the nano-fertiliser quantum dots. Nonetheless, the fresh shoot weight of ‘low nitrogen’ growth was almost identical to ‘high nitrogen’ growth, which were 1.88 g and 2.20 g, respectively. These findings epitomise the potential of the nano fertiliser to reduce nitrogen use in plants. The results from the best experimental data gathered (greenness, shoots/roots, leaf number/shape) were implemented in a visualisation of an early-stage idea for the Spaceship interior using an artificial intelligence (AI) SDXL 1.0 model. Plant growth experiments in a home-built spacecraft replica confirmed that the use of LN (0.5 mM) conditions can be favourably used under ‘real-space conditions’ and that the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot nanofertiliser is effective for creating biophilic SPAD value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Plants morphology and appearance in Space will not only be used for nutrition but also to create comfort for astronauts, especially when being isolated on a long-time space trip to Mars. On Earth, using plants to support well-being of humans are part of biophilic design principles (Firth and Jayadas, 2022). Humans capture and analyse the information from the surroundings through the five senses, however over 70 % of the surroundings are perceived visually (Zhang et al., 2020). Foliage colours can be regarded as visual stimuli, and their physiological and psychological advantages were evidenced by a reduction of oxy-Hb concentration in the frontal lobe. Choi et al. (2016). On the one hand was assumed that the fertilization (N stress) will impact the leaves greenness (Li et al., 2020). On the other hand, the human preference for foliated, green scenes with or without colourful flowers rather than dormant or senescent foliage (Kuper, 2015). The fertilisation impacted the appearance of the biophilic plant because under low nitrogen conditions, the SPAD values were lower than in higher nitrogen conditions. Nitrogen nutritional status can be associated with the greenness of leaves (Pagola et al., 2009) minerals availability and deficiencies, the main one to be nitrogen and potassium as their deficiency can result in leaf chlorosis, small leaves, reduced plant height and growth, low biomass, yield and quality (Souri et al., 2018; Souri and Hatamian, 2019). Consequently, the visual aspects of plant such as their color, shape, and overall appearance play a crucial role in creating a comforting and calming atmosphere for astronauts in isolated, confined, and often stressful Space habitats. It is therefore essential to understand plants' appearance and perception when incorporating them in Space environments. On Earth, this concept aligns with the principles of ‘biophilic design’ which emphasizes integrating natural elements into human environments to enhance well-being (Firth and Jayadas, 2022). Generally, participants preferred biophilic plant with green leaves, which are associated with healthy plants, and a good environment (Gerstenberg and Hofmann, 2016). Foliar fertilization (FF) (or foliar feeding) involves applying fertilizer directly to a plant's leaves instead of feeding the soil, which provides essential nutrients that support growth and health of plants, or as a main way to combat mineral deficiencies when soil physical and biochemical conditions restrict plant root growth and uptake (Aslani and Souri, 2018; Souri et al., 2017) enhancing biophilic effects. However, the amount of nutrients applied to the soil is higher than the amount supplied to the leaves (Singh et al., 2023). The efficiency of FF treatment can reduce the lag time between application and uptake by the plant (Gagne et al., 2019). FF nitrogen application can prolong the greenness of leaves due to chlorophyll augmentation (Singh et al., 2023) in a number of plants including mung beans (Mondal et al., 2011), corn (Rao et al., 2002), wheat, rice (Abd Kharim et al., 2019; Alim et al., 2023), and paddy (Abd. Kharim et al., 2023).or as a main way to combat minerals deficiencies when soil physical and biochemical conditions restricts plant root growth and uptake(Aslani and Souri, 2018; Souri et al., 2017). Older leaves are considered less receptive to FF treatment than younger ones (Gutiérrez-Gamboa et al., 2022). Plants cannot exploit solar energy due to the chloroplasts' inherent ability to utilise the visible light spectrum (400–700 nm wavelength range) for photosynthesis. Importantly, however, the ultraviolet (UV) light (below 350 nm), or far-red (above 650 nm wavelength) represents 10–20 % of the total sunlight energy, and cannot be used in the photosynthesis process. Therefore, from a biotechnology perspective, to expand plants' capacity to use the energy available from solar energy via artificial hybrid photosynthesis systems may be possible with Carbon dots (Guirguis et al., 2023; Li et al., 2021; White and Gardea-Torresdey, 2018).

Hydroponics and vertical farming are proposed solutions for space farming and can be combined with aeroponics, spraying of fertilisers on the leaves to upgrade the hydroponic outcome. The suspended pot with a non-circulating hydroponic method offers an advantage of reduced equipment and energy costs but it requires an initial application of a non-circulating nutrient solution that must be sufficient to supply the growth period. Equipment-wise, the effort is kept low, without the need for energy supply and pumping (Acharya et al., 2021; Arancon et al., 2019; Croft et al., 2017; Kratky, 2003; Mahlangu et al., 2016), has the potential to be used in space (Mungin et al., 2019). FF applications for medicinal and aromatic plants have demonstrated significant increase of crop yield, resistance to insects, pests and diseases, drought tolerance, and crop quality (Shahrajabian et al., 2022; Świerczyński and Bosiacki, 2022). Conventional fertiliser application can be reduced by half when supplementing it by FF without compromising plant growth and with potential to increase the fresh weight and leaf area (Świerczyński and Bosiacki, 2022). A study determined the impact of N doses through repeated FF on wheat (Ferrari et al., 2021). The nitrogen load was reduced by 25–40 %, maintaining high yield and quality. In collaboration with NASA, a study proof of concept demonstrated that spray cooling is effective in microgravity, (“Zero-gravity team finds spray cooling works in space,” n.d.). Under microgravity, a nasal spray was developed by NASA for treating motion sickness (“NASA Signs Agreement to Develop Nasal Spray for Motion Sickness - NASA,” n.d.) a design for spray cooling was reported by (Baysinger et al., 2004). To enforce resilience of biophilic plants in space, plant stressors related to space effects need to be analysed and counter measured.

Model plants have been used in plant research for their more straightforward genetics, well-established experimental protocols, extensive genomic resources, gene regulatory pathways, growth patterns, physiology, and short generation times (Druka et al., 2011). In this project, barley was selected as a model plant, which was implemented in a built model of the International Space Station (ISS) module. The study was inspired by a study by Firth and Jayadas (2022) who carried out research on biophilic design for the ISS and found that the participants' emotions were more pessimistic about the current crew quarters and more positive about the biophilic environment. The 3D rendering approach has also been exploited for immersive virtual reality (VR) studies of various types of environment (Emamjomeh, 2019; Kim and Gero, 2022; Yasar, 2023; Zhong et al., 2023) as listed in Table 1 . In essence, this project will involve using data from plant growth to render the spaceship for the crew's comfort.

The aim of this study is to achieve a high greenness of the leaves and large biomass of visible (and edible) plant parts, specifically shoots. We deliberately did not, as first step, use a traditional biophilic plant; instead, barley was chosen as a model plant (Flavell, 2009) (Fig. 1 ), specifically a genetically modified in-house dwarf variety to investigate whether plant dwarfs align with the biophilic principles of the Savannah hypothesis. For the barley growth, advanced plant-growth technologies considered for space environments, including hydroponics and aeroponics, as well as in-house frontier concepts of plant nutrition via N-nanofertilisers, are investigated. Subsequently, biophilic designs of spacecraft and space habitats can be developed by virtue of computer simulation based on the best experimental results achieved for N-triggered plant growth. This allows the rendering of a biophilic spaceship design to demonstrate the advances of the plant growth reported here. The results obtained from barley experiments will be translated to real plants exhibiting similar greenness values. It will be desirable to have more tillers and more aboveground biomass to confer an expended shape in connection with the savannah hypothesis. Numerous features of plant, such as physiology morphology, biochemical metabolism (Souri, 2022), and resource allocation are changed over levels of lighting conditions and resource allocation, differ with the level of irradiance to which plants are acclimated (Beatrice et al., 2021; Gommers et al., 2013; Shafiq et al., 2021). Meinen et al. (2018) suggested to grow lettuce for a space mission assumably in a limited space, at a lower light intensity (300 μmol m−2 s−1) rather than 600 μmol m−2 s−1 due to the leafy greens deterioration at high light intensity. Plant growth is also affected by the spectral composition of light (Avgoustaki et al., 2020; Balázs et al., 2022; Dutta Gupta and Agarwal, 2017), Klem et al. (2019) reported that different light spectra can significantly change how biomass is allocated between the above-ground and below-ground parts of barley plants, and it is a complex system. In this study, biophilic plants were grown indoors to mirror the artificial biophilic lighting, implementing biophilic approaches in indoor closed environments as part of the space architecture.

All reagents used were analytical grade. The reagents were for the macro elements, including NH4NO3, NaH2PO4.H2O, K2SO4, CaCl2.2H2O, and MgSO4.7H2O, and for the microelements, including MnSO4, H3BO3, ZnSO4, CuSO4, (NH4)6/Mo7O24 × 4H2O, and FeSO4.7H2O. The MES hydrate (C6H13NO4S) was used as buffer. The reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

The 20 seeds were placed on the Petri dish with filter paper and water with 8 ml of deionised water distributed evenly around the surface. The petri dishes were covered with aluminium and placed in the growth for three days.

After germination, the seedlings were transferred to the hydroponics for four days in the deionised water. Subsequently, after one week, the plants were grown in non-circulating hydroponic conditions for four weeks with 6 replicates, Fig. 2 . Each hydroponic contains a volume of 350 ml of the hydroponic solution. The roots of the 3-day germinated seedling were suspended. To prevent the foam from becoming overwetted, a distance of at least 5 cm was maintained between the water surface and the foam's bottom.

The samples were placed in the hydroponic cup and arranged randomly following the Latin square randomisation design. The 1/2 Tadano and Tanaka medium was the hydroponic solution, and the concentration of N was adjusted as nutrient stress. The roots of the 3-day germinated seedling were suspended, as shown in Fig. 2. To prevent the foam from becoming overwetted, a distance of at least 5 cm was maintained between the water surface and the foam's bottom.

The concentration of macroelements for NaH2PO4, K2SO4, CaCl2, and MgSO4 were 0.16 mM, 1 mM, 1 mM and 1 mM, respectively. The concentration of microelements MnSO4, H3BO3, ZnSO4, CuSO4, (NH4)6/Mo7O24 were 0.02 mM, 0.01 mM, 0.003 mM, 0.0002 mM and 0.00005 mM, respectively. The MES hydrate was used as a buffer, and the pH of the ½ TT medium was adjusted to 6 with KOH before putting the plants. The solution was changed every week. Several experiments were done for non-circulating hydroponics, including fertiliser conditions (High nitrogen (HN), low nitrogen (LN)), various FF conditions, and use of conventional and NCDOTS nanofertilisers, Table 2 .

The plants were grown in a growth room chamber under a constant temperature of 250 C and a photoperiod of 16 h of light and 8 h of darkness. The photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) was 120 μmol m−2 s−1. The control samples were grown under Specific N conditions and were sprayed with deionised water with twin 20 as a wetting agent or surfactant, and the FF treatment solution was sprayed with a solution containing 2 mM of NH4NO3 or 10 mg/l of NCDOTS synthesised by gram scale by (Pho et al., 2022), Fig. 3 . The characterisation via a point beam EDX analysis disclosed, three significant peaks of carbon (44.6 %), oxygen (29.8 %), and nitrogen (25.5 %) in function of atomic mass (Pho et al., 2023). The full XPS exhited three peaks at 285.5 eV, 400.3 eV, and 532.8 eV, s that NCDOTS is composed of C, N, and O elements, and the atomic percentages were calculated to 57 %, 13 %, and 30 %, respectively (Pho et al., 2022).The photoluminescence of the dissolved NCDOTS as FF solution was carried out using the FLS1000 apparatus to assess the surfactant's influence on the nanofertiliser's fluorescence.

Each FF volume was evaluated to be approximately 3 ml except when 1 ml of the NCDOTS with a concentration of 100 mg/l were poured directly into the hydroponic solution. The weight of the NCDOTS, 0.1 mg, was deducted from equation (1).

Where c, m and v are the mass concentration (g/l), weight (g) and volume (l), respectively.

The SPAD reading and biomass allocation were collected when the plants were 4 weeks old. The SPAD data were measured with the Apogee CCM 200 plus device, and the reported value was determined as the average of the replicates on the second youngest leaves (from the top).

The SPAD values were calculated as described previously by (Yuan et al., 2016) Equation (2):

where f (SPADi) is the mean SPAD value of the sample; the SPAD values of SPADi were recorded for each replicate.

In this study, it was desirable to have a higher shoot-to-root ratio for attractiveness that can confer a biophilic feature in line with the savannah hypothesis. The fresh biomass allocation was determined by weighing the fresh shoots and roots with the scale.

The biomass allocation was expressed as a function of shoot-to-root ratio (S/R) equation (3).

The statistical analysis of the row (N conditions) and the column (samples) were proceeded with two ways Anova (P < 0.05). The analysis proceeded with the software GraphPad Prism 10.1.0. The sample size was (n = 6). The variables were SPAD, biomass allocation, and S/R ratio.

While this study does not intend to develop the interior design of an ISS, it intends to follow the footsteps of similar immersive virtual reality (VR) studies conducted by others (e.g. Emamjomeh, 2019; Kim and Gero, 2022; Yasar, 2023; Zhong et al., 2023) by exploring the implementation of the plant growth results from the study to the biophilic design for the ISS. The Red, Green, and Blue (RGB) values of the plants were extracted from the LLC image designated as the scanned leaf colour. The data retrieved from the leaf colour were applied to simulate the perception of the lush garden on the spaceship concept from Axiom Space with 3D rendering.

Previous studies have supported the correlation between the SPAD and the leaf LCC value (Fig. 4 ) in diverse plants such as rice (Duttarganvi et al., 2014; Johnkutty et al., 2000), wheat (Barad et al., 2018; Ghosh et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022), maize (Chen et al., 2023; Thind et al., 2011), and barley (Xu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). The perception of the foliage's indoor greenness can influence the cognitive capacity of the occupants (Abdelaal and Soebarto, 2018; Elsadek et al., 2017; Ikei et al., 2014). Thus, this LCC can serve as a good approximation for the scenario involved in this project. The RGB values were extracted from the LCC image by averaging the colours within the indicated box, which can then be applied to the 3D rendering.

As results were taken, the greenness values were determined (SPAD), as well as the root and shoot dimensions. Space farming indoor designs were considered, like Earth greenhouses but with space architecture design. Barley, as we studied it, is considered; noting this is not a biophilic plant. Yet, as said, barley is an ideal, well-understood model plant and in space farmed plants and indoor plants are expected to “mix”; meaning that plants intended for astronaut food production will also function to support the astronaut's wellbeing. In other words, this space design study will show real biophilic plants (as on Earth) in a Space living environment, to which the results of this study have been translated.

Some inspirations for space biophilic design were generated using generative artificial intelligence (AI). Text-to-image prompts were written to guide the output towards concepts which visualised design characteristics. This study employed the Stability AI SDXL 1.0 model with CreativeML Open RAIL++-M License, which allows the output to be used commercially.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biophilic plant growth in home-built real-size spacecraft replica
ce:section.ce:para: Biophilic plants were also grown in biophilic modules placed on a home-built real-size spacecraft replica (Fig. 5 ). The latter was developed by a consortium, including authors of this study, and headed by the School of Architecture and Civil Engineering to mimic the indoor biophilic plant space conditions.

The light was sunlight white (full spectrum), warm white (380–800 nm), and red and blue light (460–600 nm) (Fig. 6 ). This commercial lighting is abbreviated in the following as ‘warm, full-sun spectrum, and red & blue’. Note that 1x3 head LED grow light with an adaptable tripod was supplied by lamp manufacturer Suwitu LED. Basil and sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum) were grown in the space biophilic modules. The biophilic plants were grown for 4 weeks under a lower PAR compared to the biophilic growth in the growth chamber, as described in Sections 2.3 and 2.4. The PAR intensity was 32.9, 17.2, and 39.0, respectively. In subsequent sections, the type of light will be referred to as light 1, 2 and 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of spraying after days of sowing (DAS)
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments with the two-week samples were done under the hypothesis that too early spraying (in week one) might have deteriorating effects on the plant growth, e.g., lead to “leaf burning” (Peirce et al., 2019; Starast et al., 2001). Compared to the HN, the FF treatment with NH4NO3 at 14 DAS increased the tillering, and the lowest tiller number was observed under LN, Fig. 7 . This tendency can be attributed to the influence of nitrogen on plants' tillering (Chadhokar and Humphreys, 1973; Luo, 2022; Wang et al., 2017).Seeing the results and those of the one-week sample, this hypothesis is not confirmed, meaning it was determined that FF should start as early as possible. Although younger leaves can be prone to fertiliser burn than older leaves (Johnson, 2018). It can be inferred based on the wax and the cuticle presence on the surface of the leave (Husted et al., 2023). if younger leaves have more wax than older leaves, then applying fertilisers at earlier growth stages when the flag leaf is not fully emerged may lead to reduced foliar uptake than at the later growth stages. Conversely, if younger leaves have less wax than older leaves, then applying fertilisers at earlier growth stages when the flag leaf is not fully emerged, may in turn lead to higher foliar uptake (Peirce et al., 2014). Furthermore, the cuticle is a barrier to the penetration of hydrophilic compounds in the leaves; the thicker it is, the greater the barrier will be. Since young leaves possess a thin cuticle, hydrophilic compounds are more likely to penetrate than older leaves. (Romacier, 1966).

The height increased when the plants were treated at 7 DAS compared to 14 DAS, the mean value control treatment 29.2 cm–36.5 cm under 1 mM, which is statistically significant (P value = 0.0495 < 0.05); this trend can be ascribed to the N availability (Perthame et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021). Similarly, under the analogous conditions, the SPAD increased slightly from 25.1 to 31.1 when the FF treatment started at 7 DAS rather than 14 DAS. The opposite effect was observed when the FF treatment started at 14 DAS. This trend can be ascribed to older leaves being deemed less receptive than younger leaves to FF treatment (Gutiérrez-Gamboa et al., 2022).

The fresh shoot was higher when the FF treatment started at 7 DAS and increased approximately for a quarter (27 %). Turley and Ching (1986) reported an increase of almost one-third of biomass shoot weight in barley after three weekly sprays as a response to FF N application. As part of this study, the increase in fresh weight might increase the biophilic outcome, for instance the bushiness features related to the expanded shape. Nonetheless, the FF treatment was inefficient in the case of 14 DAS and LN.

The fresh root decreased when the FF treatment started late. For 14 DAS, it was 0.56g compared to 1.46 g for 7 DAS. This difference was statistically significant for the 14 DAS experiment (P value = 0.0207 < 0.05). Reciprocally, the fresh shoot-root ratio was higher for the FF when started at 7 DAS. Overall, the dry biomass was higher for the FF 7 DAS. This can be due to the younger leaves being more receptive to the N FF treatment (Gutiérrez-Gamboa et al., 2022). The FF treatment can be effective from 7 DAS rather than 14 DAS.

As expected from the reported dependency of SPAD and biomass, the amount of aboveground biomass (shoots) compared to belowground biomass (roots) shows a similar trend under different nitrogen conditions. The difference between HN and LN conditions, as well as between the control and FF samples, is less significant (P value = 0.3480 > 0.05) than for the SPAD value. This implies that in spaceship and space habitats a decision can be made between choosing high greenness using HN conditions or fertiliser-biomass efficiency using LN conditions.

After selecting the DAS for FF treatment, in this section, the experiments started from 7 DAS. The FF treatment with carbon dots was applied once and twice a week to improve the greenness of the plants when the nitrogen concentrations in the hydroponic solution were 0.2 mM, 0.5 mM, and 1 mM, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . As a result, the FF treatment did not promote the tillering formation under 1 mM regardless of the frequency of the FF application; nonetheless, with a twice FF treatment under 0.5 mM N, the tillers numbers reached the one under 1 mM with a twice FF treatment weekly, the lowest concentration 0.2 mM did not promote any tiller formation regardless the samples (control or FF samples).

The FF treatment cannot increase shoot biomass under HN and LN conditions. Shoot and root biomass are practically the same for the spray and control experiment under LN conditions, with better results for the control experiment under HN conditions. The height increased with N concentration due to N availability; the tallest samples were recorded under 1 mM, followed by 0.5 mM, and the shortest was 0.2 mM. There was a slight difference between the mock (spray with water) and the FF treatment with NCDOTS, which was not statistically significant (P value = 0.7564 > 0.05). The nitrogen concentration in the leaves did not improve, as reflected by the SPAD value; the lowest concentration was recorded at 0.2 mM. However, the SPAD was slightly different under 0.5 mM N and 1 mM N.

The FF treatment frequency influenced the fresh shoot weight; for instance, under 1 mM, it increased from 1.34 g to 2.07 g for the sample sprayed once and twice, respectively; the value was below the mock sample. Conversely, the FF treatment decreased the fresh shoot under 0.5 mM N, where spraying twice decreased the fresh shoot weight from 1.90 g when sprayed once to 1.55 g for the later experiments. Under 0.2 mM, the FF treatment did not have a perceptible impact. Similarly, the fresh below-ground biomass weight decreased when the FF treatment was carried out twice weekly under 1 mM and increased when the FF treatment was carried out once weekly under 0.5 mM.

As reported in previous studies, the dry biomass weight below ground was the highest at under 0.2 mM due to the plants' survival mechanism of investing more in the root under nutrient stress. (Francis et al., 2023; Gull et al., 2019; Mohammed et al., 2022).

Except under 1 mM N, the dry s/r of the mock and the sprayed twice weekly were almost equal, and the mean value was 3.76 for the former and 3.82 for the latter. Overall, the dry s/r for the sample sprayed with the carbon nanodots was inferior to the mock, and the sample sprayed twice with nitrogen carbon exhibited the highest s/r value, except under 0.2 mM.

The SPAD results are slightly better than for use of the FF once per week at HN conditions. The SPAD results are much better than for use of the FF once per week at LN conditions, where the need of getting extra fertiliser is higher. The SPAD values under LN conditions are also much better than for the use of nanofertilisers. Based on the observation of the biophilic criteria of the savannah hypothesis that the lowest tiller numbers were recorded for the N conditions of 0.2 mM, to promote the feature of expanded shape for a bushinesh features, the biomass allocation varied with spray frequency, the fresh S/R for were 2.1 (P Value = 0.001 < 0.05) for spraying once weekly and decreased to 1.1 for spraying twice weekly under the same N concentration of 0.5 mM, and it was 1.80 for the control sample under 1 mM.

This experiment aimed to assess the efficiency of the NCDOTs fertiliser between the FF treatment or the root application route, Fig. 9 . The tiller numbers were almost the same regardless of the treatment and the N concentration in the solution; the height and the SPAD followed the same trend. The independent variables aforementioned were not statistically significant with P value = 0.6016 > 0.05 and P value = 0.3022 > 0.05.

The fresh shoot weight of the sample treated with nitrogen-carbon dots fertiliser application was less than the mock under 1 mM; conversely, the shoot weight increased under LN when the carbon dots solution was tipped in the hydroponic solution. The mean weight was 1,88g, 14 % lower than the mock under 1 mM (2,20g). The fresh root was higher under 1 mM for the FF and under 0.5 mM when the carbon dots fertiliser was tipped in the solution. Li et al. (2021) reported that carbon dots' performance could be influenced by hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CD surfaces involved in the production of cyclic nucleotides and cyclic nucleotide-gated ion channels, which act as Ca2+ signal amplifiers and ROS scavengers. The median dry shoot weight of the mock was higher under 1 mM N 0.52 g, and under 0.5 mM, the median weight for the FF sample was slightly higher: 0.40 g, 0.37 g and 0.39 g for the mock and NCDOTS in the solution samples. The mean dry root weight was the highest under 0.5 mM when the carbon dots fertiliser was tipped in the hydroponic solution. The dry s/r was almost identical under HN, and the FF sample was marginally higher under LN.
The presence of the surfactant Tween 20 did not impede the fluorescence emission of the NCDOTS at 450 nm, Fig. 10 (Pho et al., 2023).

Four scenarios were considered to define biophilic plants.
•
‘High SPAD’, based on the highest SPAD value in this study for fertiliser sprays,
•
‘Control’, as defined above (spray without fertilisers), and with best SPAD value,
•
‘Low SPAD’, based on the lowest SPAD value in this study for fertiliser sprays,
•
‘Ideal Biophilic Plant’, based on extrapolated ‘High SPAD’ to full plant growth after several weeks; noting all three above cases represent four weeks' growth period.
The RGB of the biophilic plants aforementioned are recorded in Fig. 11 .

Plants of the first three scenarios have the same geometry for consistency, while the ‘Ideal Biophilic Plant’, with its more developed growth stage, has more leaves, Fig. 12 . The more tillers, the more the plants look bushy and expanded.

The spacing between plants has been kept the same for all four scenarios. The methodology defines the RGB value as derived from the LCC panel value, Fig. 11. When the corresponding LCC panel value falls between two, the midpoint of both RGB values was calculated and used. Table 3 provides a summary of all input parameters taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualised support of future space biophilic design
ce:section.ce:para: This study can provide crucial insight on future space biophilic design by visualising how the plant growth results would transition into a very initial-stage of Space biophilic design. A full developed architectural design would have to demonstrate the appropriateness of the proposed design by comparison studies, including by consulting experts, or asking the perception of research participants where they are shown these images. fundamental design theory about designing spaces would have to be applied, including such as theories on ‘proportion’, ‘contrast’, ‘function’, ‘spatial requirements’, ‘ergonomic’, etc.

Within those limits, this research supports the creation of a better interior of a spaceship that should be designed based on biophilic-design principles in order to improve the wellbeing of the astronauts. This study was able to come up with plants with certain characteristics that meet the principles of biophilic design. Those improvements are visualised within the capacity and limitations of above-mentioned ‘initial-stage space biophilic design’, considering some obvious space-farm design considerations, as follows:
•
Spread the greenhouse throughout the Space station instead of concentrating it in one area
•
To use the greenhouse as a substitute for window views, or to enhance the views of Earth
•
To take inspiration from manicured garden designs, such as the Japanese and French gardens
•
To allow unhindered interaction and access to the greenhouse
•
An open concept where the crew can touch the plants directly
•
To have an immersive and multisensory experience of nature
•
To have indoor lights which replicates natural light in terms of visual appearance and variable (circadian rhythm)
•
To allow access to greenery from the crews' private spaces

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualisation of this study's results towards future space-farm designs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the experimentally determined SPAD (greenness) value of the leaves and the root and shoot biomasses, this study developed 3D models and digital renderings of plant growth for initial visualisation towards future design of space farms, which might potentially be part of space habitats or spacecrafts (Fig. 13 ) shows a design visualisation based on the results of this study and the four scenarios defined above. The variation in SPAD and the benefit of advanced plant growth (control versus FF), determined in this study, can be clearly expressed in a visually recognisable difference in the greenness (biophilic) experience. The exploration towards a full (several months) growth of the barley plant shows a visibly recognisable improvement beyond the three other scenarios, demonstrating that going to full plant growth improves the biophilic value.

As given in our motivation, this study takes inspiration from manicured garden design, as being well advanced in Japanese or French gardens (Fig. 14 ). The mere food-production oriented space farm designs of Fig. 13 above are translated to a ‘space garden’ design shown in Fig. 14, while maintaining the key design characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualisation of this study's results with real biophilic plants – the ‘living room’ of astronauts
ce:section.ce:para: Evidently, barley and other food-making crops do not have the same biophilic value as real biophilic plants on Earth have demonstrated. This study also developed a biophilic design of a ‘living room’ for astronauts, based on the assumption of having the ‘luxury’ to disregard economic viabilities and technical limitations. In these outputs, key design characteristics were showcased and a large variety of biophilic plants were present. These inspired the above-mentioned space farm and ‘space garden’ design concepts (Fig. 15 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biophilic plant growth inside a realistic replica of a spacecraft architecture
ce:section.ce:para: A realistic replica (of real dimensions) of a spacecraft architecture has been installed at our site for astronaut well-being and psychological studies. In this study, the spacecraft replica has been used to let plants grow in biophilic modules that are positioned on a vertical wall at the end of the spacecraft replica (allowing the astronaut to face it that way). LED lights are positioned in proximate contact to the plants on this wall. The research question of this last objective of this study is can plants be grown under ‘real-space biophilic conditions’ (rather than in one of the several enclosed environmental systems); meaning in a kind of “space garden”. It includes to test if the central plant intensification concepts of this study immediately apply. To note, this last part of the study is a ‘research outlook’ and is meant to motivate another detailed study to be reported in future.

The plant growth was investigated under the three types of light, as described in Section 2.8, under the growth of scenario 6 given in Table 2; meaning the N-doped carbon dot nanofertilisers were used (without foliar spray). Supplement biophilic plants were grown in the spacecraft replica during a period of four weeks. Basil, as an ornamental plant, and sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum) were grown in the space biophilic modules due to their potential of human psychological benefits for the astronaut crew as reported for the plant atrium system for NASA (Massa et al., 2013) and the demonstrated benefits of aromatics in space (Rodman, 2005).

Several plant growth parameters were investigated to assess the basil growth. First, the tiller development was taken. The biophilic plant grown under the full spectrum exhibited two tillers; the plants grown under different N concentrations and light conditions were similar (Fig. 16 ).

Best results were obtained for the control experiment under the full spectrum (light 1) which resulted in the highest biomass compared to the treated (nanofertilised) samples and to the other types of light. This control experiment resulted also in the highest shoot weight. The use of nanofertilisers is effective in reaching best SPAD values, which is the essential asset of biophilicity. The use of low nitrogen fertiliser supply (LN) gives competitive results to the use of high nitrogen fertiliser supply (HN). Only the overall biomass, as expected, is somewhat lower, meaning the shoots and roots are less developed, but the root-to-shoot (S/R) ratio stays the same.

Sweet basil (Ocimum basilicum) plant growth turned out to be even more discriminative. The dominance of light 1 in shoot weight and S/R ratio is apparent. LN conditions are competitive or even better than HN conditions. Sweet basil grown under the red and blue light under HN were the shortest; conversely, they were taller under LN (Fig. 17 ).

The inferior performance of the innovations of this study for biophilic plant growth in the spacecraft, as compared to the other results in this study, does not come out of surprise. The spacecraft replica is designed for astronauts to live and work. It is not an ‘optimal glasshouse’ and never will be. It has to use a lower light intensity, as photosynthetically active radiation (PAR), in order not to bedazzle the astronauts. Yet, the net result is that important features such as the SPAD value and the effectiveness of LN conditions can be transitioned from best-operated laboratory conditions towards a realistic spacecraft replica.

The experiments involved the plants' stress and biofortification with varying nitrogen (N) nutrient content; high nitrogen (HN) and low nitrogen (LN). The dwarf plants that grew under different N conditions in non-circulating hydroponics had different attributes associated with the Savannah hypothesis for instance the extended shape and bushiness of the aboveground biomass. The SPAD and the fresh shoots' weight decreased as the N concentration decreased. This trend was ascribed to the propensity of the plants to invest in underground biomass under N stress. The FF treatment was more efficient under HN than LN, improving the shoots-to ratio by 15 %. The improvement of shoot weight under LN (0.5 mM N) was observed when 1 ml of the nitrogen-doped carbon nanofertiliser (NCDOTS) was added to the hydroponic solution. The fresh shoots' weight was approximately equal. However, the S/R 1.16 was lower than the HN (1 mM N) sample 1.83. The implementation of the rendering using the AI SDXL 1.0 model unveils an opportunity to apply the specific greenness and bushy features of the dwarf model plants for prospective benefits not only for human cognitive capacity from a biophilic point of view in a closed and small environment such as an Axiom Space spacecraft but also for the implementation of a lush garden that can attenuate the uncomfortable glare emanating from space lighting. Plant growth experiments in a home-built spacecraft replica confirmed that the use of LN conditions can be favourably used under ‘real-space conditions’ and that the NCDOTS nanofertiliser is effective for creating biophilic SPAD value.

John Masengo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jun Hong Cheong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software. Nguyen Van Duc Long: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Veronica Soebarto: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ranjan Swarup: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.
